title: The ties that bind: Appreciation for ADC linkers
text:
Produced by

An ADC showing a monoclonal antibody and its payloads, with the all-important linkers in between.Credit: Lonza In the world of antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) linkers are the unsung heroes. First, they marry hydrophilic (attracted to water) antibodies to payloads that are often water-repellent (hydrophobic). Once inside the body, they maintain that connection until the conjugate enters a cell. Then, and only then, they dissolve completely, releasing their payloads to start working. Yet, for all their essential functions, linkers are often overlooked. “When people think of ADCs, they think about the antibody’s targeting effect and the cytotoxic payload,” says Charles Johnson, senior director of commercial development at Lonza, a contract development and manufacturing organization (CDMO). “So yes, we have an antibody and payload, but that doesn’t mean our problems are solved — far from it.” Some of those problems include hydrophobicity, poor pharmacokinetics and aggregation. As companies begin scaling their ADCs, Johnson suggests they pay closer attention to their linkers sooner, to ensure safety, stability and efficacy. Good linkers make good neighbours Because ADC payloads can be toxic, safety is a critical issue. Strong linkers maintain ADC stability in the bloodstream, ensuring the conjugate doesn’t release its payload too soon and cause serious off-target effects. Yet, this union is not forever. Once the ADC enters its target cell, the lysosome eats the linker’s peptides and the medicinal payload is released. “It’s incredibly important that the linker system degrades in the presence of certain lysosomal enzymes, but not in circulation,” says Johnson. “What’s more, the linker must not just separate, it has to self-destruct. Leaving even a stub on the payload could dampen its efficacy.” The linker plays another key role by modulating the ADC’s relationship with water. A hydrophobic payload can negatively affect the ADC’s pharmacokinetics, limiting its medicinal benefits. Hydrophobic patches can also make ADCs aggregate and attract the immune system’s attention1. Drug manufacturers often coat linkers with hydrophilic compounds, such as polysarcosine or polyethylene glycol (PEG), to counteract the payload’s hydrophobicity and get closer to the antibody’s natural hydrophilicity2. In this way, a hydrophilic coating supports ADC efficacy and safety. “PEG and polysarcosine provide a shielding hydrophilic effect, which is incredibly important for certain payloads,” says Johnson. “Traditional cytotoxic molecules are fairly hydrophobic; protein degraders can be extremely hydrophobic. On the other hand, oligonucleotides, like siRNAs, are quite hydrophilic. So, linker design is heavily governed by payload.” This dependency drives a logical ADC development progression. First, payloads should be selected based on which agents work in specific indications. “The payload informs the drug-to-antibody ratio (DAR), which affects stability, pharmacokinetics, aggregation and ultimately efficacy,” says Johnson. “With a traditional cytotoxic payload, we know how much hydrophobicity we need to mitigate through the linker.” ADCs with chemistry Working with ADCs is challenging at any level, but particularly at scale. During early-stage efforts, labs handle tiny quantities of payload–linkers. As drug development proceeds, those numbers will increase dramatically: hundreds of grams and ultimately kilos of material. “We can't handle these quantities in a normal lab, we need specialized assets,” says Johnson. “We have to contain them in an appropriate way because the payloads are somewhere between a hundred and a thousand times more potent than standard chemotherapeutic agents.” Managing toxic molecules safely is just one potential complication. Introducing PEG or polysarcosine to linkers means labs are no longer dealing with crystalline molecules, making payload–linkers difficult to purify3. To overcome this, companies must embrace chromatography, which throws up its own challenges. “Chromatography needs to be applied multiple times to keep the purity up, even just for the payload–linker system,” says Johnson. “A kilo of material can produce 11 metric tonnes of solvent waste, which is not a sustainable process.” How to improve ADCs As complex as ADC development can be, there are emerging methods that could vastly simplify the process. “Traditional ADCs have a long way to go to optimize their impact,” says Johnson, “and one of the greatest areas in innovation is in link tech.” Developed by Synaffix, a Lonza company, a technology called GlycoConnect® shifts how linkers are attached to antibodies, enzymatically trimming the antibody’s native glycans to attach them4. Another of Lonza’s technologies, HydraSpace®, adds a hydrophilic linker component to promote safety, stability and ease of conjugation, as well as counteracting hydrophobicity5. These technologies can potentially enhance solubility, reduce aggregation and boost pharmacokinetics. Artificial intelligence (AI) is another emerging force in ADC development. More robust testing of potential conjugates in silico could alleviate much of the downstream trial and error that slows development and increases costs. In addition, AI-powered route scouting is already helping manufacturers optimize development, combining synthesis and supply chain data to identify the most cost-effective choices. “AI is really coming to the forefront with ADCs and is being used to engineer the antibodies, optimize payload–linker traits and perform other critical tasks,” says Johnson. “It won’t be a substitute for really understanding the chemistry and the various pitfalls that can come during development, but it has tremendous potential to accelerate the process.” While ADCs are incredibly complex drugs, many of the risks associated with their development are foreseeable — if biopharma companies understand what they should be looking for. In some cases, they may understand the biological issues, such as developing the right antibody to hit a specific target, but fall short on the chemistry side, which plays a crucial role in linkers, purification and scaling. Above all, says Johnson, when it comes to ADCs, there’s no substitute for experience.
